description: Replacing an index used by the hot-path ad-serving query. The replacement adds a covering column. Review the timing strategy and exposure during the change.
review_checklist: Identify the time window when the table has no usable index for the hot query
Verify CONCURRENTLY usage on both DROP and CREATE
Audit the order of operations for online migration
Consider whether the new index actually helps the target query

ground_truth.description: DROP INDEX idx_imp_campaign_served BEFORE creating the replacement leaves the table without the supporting index for the hot ad-serving query. With 4B rows, queries that previously hit the index now seqscan — at 50K queries/sec, the database melts within seconds. Correct order: (1) CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY new_index, (2) verify it's valid + planner uses it, (3) DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY old_index. Atomic from the application's perspective.

DROP INDEX without CONCURRENTLY acquires AccessExclusiveLock on the table. On a 4B-row table at 50K qps, even the brief lock causes a query queue to build up; with serving-tier timeouts of 50ms, cascading failures hit upstream services within seconds. Use DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

CREATE INDEX without CONCURRENTLY on 4B rows. Builds a SHARE lock on the table for hours. All writes block. Combined with the prior DROP, the table is effectively dead during the migration. Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

INCLUDE (cost_micros) on a covering index means each index entry stores cost_micros. With a 4B-row table this adds ~32GB to the index. Verify the target query actually benefits — if cost_micros is rarely needed in the hot path, leaving it out of the index keeps writes faster and saves cache.

Architectural critique: ad_impressions at 4B rows with no partitioning is a ticking time bomb. Daily growth + index updates will eventually outpace VACUUM. Partition by served_at (RANGE, monthly) — old partitions become read-only, indexes only built once, drop entire partitions for archival. Combined with BRIN indexes on time-series queries.

Architectural critique: PG is not optimal storage for ad-serving at 50K qps. Consider tiered storage: live serving from Redis/Aerospike key-value with TTL, batch flush to PG for analytics, or move analytics to ClickHouse / Druid. PG remains a system of record but isn't on the serving hot path.
